perturbation.original_text: In accordance with Rule 13d-1(k) under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended, the undersigned hereby confirm the agreement by and among them to the joint filing on behalf of them of the Statement on Schedule 13D and any and all further amendments thereto, with respect to the securities of the above referenced issuer, and that this Agreement be included as an Exhibit to such filing.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding Rule 13d-1(k) under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended, the undersigned hereby confirm the agreement by and among them to the joint filing on behalf of them of the Statement on Schedule 13G and any and all further amendments thereto, with respect to only certain securities of the above referenced issuer, and that this Agreement be included as an internal document. This agreement will be only for internal purposes.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text creates an in-text contradiction by changing the reference from Schedule 13D to 13G, and restricting the securities covered. It also states that the document is solely for internal use. This contradicts the original intent of filing publicly under the Exchange Act. The modified text's restriction to "certain securities" also introduces ambiguity about which securities are covered by the joint filing agreement, creating uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Notwithstanding Rule 13d-1(k) under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended, the undersigned hereby confirm the agreement by and among them to the joint filing on behalf of them of the Statement on Schedule 13G and any and all further amendments thereto, with respect to only certain securities of the above referenced issuer, and that this Agreement be included as an internal document. This agreement will be only for internal purposes.
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a contradiction because it modifies the type of filing from Schedule 13D to 13G, restricts the coverage to 'certain securities,' and designates the agreement as 'internal' only. The original text implies a public filing under the Securities Exchange Act. The changed text's limitation introduces ambiguity and directly conflicts with the expectation of a public filing, creating an in-text contradiction that could impact enforceability under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as stated in the Perturbation Explanation. The contradicted text is simply a restatement of the Changed Text, which indicates that it is a change that introduces a contradition within the text.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be executed in any number of counterparts each of which shall be deemed to be an original and all of which together shall be deemed to constitute one and the same Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may only be executed once and must be kept as a single original, with no reproductions allowed. If multiple signed versions are found, the agreement is immediately void.
perturbation.explanation: This modification creates an in-text contradiction by restricting the agreement to a single original and voiding the agreement if multiple versions exist. This directly contradicts the original provision allowing execution in multiple counterparts, which is standard practice for such agreements. The modified term will directly affect the ability of the parties to rely on signed copies of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement may only be executed once and must be kept as a single original, with no reproductions allowed. If multiple signed versions are found, the agreement is immediately void.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a direct contradiction. The original text explicitly allows for multiple counterparts of the agreement, while the changed text restricts the agreement to a single original and voids it if multiple copies exist. This directly conflicts with the contradicted text, which is identical to the changed text. The explanation provided highlights the in-text contradiction created by restricting the agreement to a single original, impacting the parties' ability to rely on signed copies.
